main_summary.summary: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew, second chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), recorded this interview on June 4, 2011 in Albany, Georgia during the 53rd Reunion of the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement. McDew describes his upbringing in Massillon, Ohio, a steel town shaped by union activism and immigrant labor, where his father played for Paul Brown's Massillon Tigers and his uncle Horace Gillom played for the Cleveland Browns. He recounts being sent to South Carolina State College in Orangeburg in fall 1959 at his father's insistence to encounter Southern Black professional life, his first exposure to legal segregation during the drive south, and three arrests over Thanksgiving 1959 in Sumter, South Carolina, including a beating by police that broke his arm and jaw. McDew narrates being elected student spokesman after the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, his refusal to swear lifelong nonviolence at the April 1960 Shaw University founding conference, his role in naming and shaping SNCC, his replacement of Marion Barry as chair, the Rock Hill jail-no-bail action, and SNCC's voter-registration strategy targeting Black Belt counties in Mississippi and Southwest Georgia to unseat segregationist senators like John Stennis, James Eastland, and Herman Talmadge.
key_themes: Voter registration strategy in the Black Belt
Northern Black childhood meets Southern segregation
main_summary.historical_significance:
McDew's account is a primary-source narration of SNCC's founding moment by the person who chaired the organization through its formative voter-registration turn (1960-1963). His articulation of the disagreement with Dr. King over lifelong nonviolence at the April 1960 Shaw conference, and his framing of nonviolence as tactic rather than creed, documents the philosophical line that separated SNCC from SCLC at the organization's birth.

The interview also preserves McDew's strategic rationale for targeting Black Belt counties: that committee chairmanships in the U.S. House and Senate were held by white Southern men whose seats depended on Black non-voting in heavily Black counties, and that registering voters in places like Dougherty, Terrell, Amite, Pike, and Walthall counties was the leverage point to dislodge senators including John Stennis, James Eastland, Robert Byrd, and Herman Talmadge. This first-person strategic framing is rare in the oral-history record.

chapters.title: Massillon Roots and Steel-Football Town
chapters.summary: McDew describes his birth on June 23, 1938 in Massillon, Ohio, a town of about 38,000 known for steel production and football. His father and grandfather played for Paul Brown's Massillon Tigers, and the Pro Football Hall of Fame in Canton sits eight miles away. His father worked for Republic Steel.

chapters.title: Union Heritage and Integrated Schools
chapters.summary: McDew recalls growing up active in United Steel Workers Local 1124, where his grandfather, uncles, and father all worked the steel mills. He describes Massillon's integrated neighborhood schools, his sister becoming one of the first Black teachers in Massillon public schools, and his father's training as a teacher that could not be used in the North before the 1954 Brown decision.
related_events: 1954 Brown v. Board of Education decision

chapters.title: Early Exposure to Race and Civil Rights
chapters.summary: McDew describes pre-South civil rights awareness: traveling from Massillon to Pittsburgh as a child to support Bobby Grier of the Pitt football team when bowl organizers refused to play against a team with a Black player; writing letters to James Lawson, minister at his mother's St. James A.M.E. Church and a conscientious objector in jail; and his uncle Horace Gillom playing for the Cleveland Browns.

chapters.title: Massillon as Immigrant America
chapters.summary: McDew frames Massillon as an expression of the American dream built by worker-immigrants from Greece, Spain, Yugoslavia, Czechoslovakia, and the American South, all coming for decent jobs, schools, and health care. He recounts hearing General Jacob Coxey himself at the barbershop, alongside grandfathers who had marched in Coxey's Army of the Poor.

chapters.title: Steel Economics and Going South to College
chapters.summary: McDew describes learning supply and demand from the rhythms of Mesabi iron ore arriving in Massillon, becoming steel, going to Detroit auto plants, then layoffs. He recounts his father's decision to send him to South Carolina State College in Orangeburg in fall 1959, against his own plan to play football at Michigan and turn pro, to expose him to a Southern Black professional class.
related_events: McDew's matriculation at South Carolina State, fall 1959

chapters.title: First Encounter with Segregation
chapters.summary: McDew describes the drive from Massillon to Orangeburg on Route 21, his father preparing him for bathroom and food segregation once they crossed into West Virginia, packed lunches because no places would feed Black travelers, and his initial paradise at South Carolina State surrounded by Black female classmates he was not related to.

chapters.title: Thanksgiving 1959: Three Arrests in Two Days
chapters.summary: McDew recounts being radicalized over Thanksgiving 1959 in Sumter, South Carolina, where his roommate Charles Gatson had taken him home. He fought back when a police officer struck him for not saying "yes sir" and was beaten bloody with broken arm and jaw, arrested for disturbing the peace, re-arrested the same evening for refusing the segregated baggage car on the train, and arrested a third time for cutting through Edisto Gardens park on a forbidden day.
related_events: McDew's Thanksgiving 1959 arrests in Sumter, SC

chapters.title: Greensboro Sit-In and Orangeburg Spokesman
chapters.summary: McDew describes being asked by South Carolina State students to be their spokesman after the February 1, 1960 sit-in by North Carolina A&T students in Greensboro. He became head of the Orangeburg Movement for Civic Improvement, was arrested delivering a petition to Governor Ernest "Fritz" Hollings in Columbia, and was arrested with roughly 800 others in Orangeburg demanding desegregation of public places.

chapters.title: Shaw Conference and SNCC Founding
chapters.summary: McDew narrates the April 1960 Shaw University conference convened by Dr. King and SCLC. He refused King's proposal that students join SCLC and accept nonviolence as a lifelong creed, arguing nonviolence was a tactic, not a way of life, and would not work against an amoral society. He and others met down the hall to form the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, with the "nonviolent" word retained at the urging of James Lawson's Nashville students.

chapters.title: SNCC Chair and Rock Hill Jail-No-Bail
chapters.summary: McDew describes electing Marion Barry as SNCC's first chairman to take the students' concerns to the 1960 political conventions, then succeeding Barry as chair in the fall. He recounts SNCC's development of the jail-no-bail tactic and its first deployment in Rock Hill, South Carolina, supported by Rev. C.A. Ivory and NAACP state head I. DeQuincey Newman.

chapters.title: Mississippi and the Black Belt Strategy
chapters.summary: McDew narrates SNCC's 1961 entry into McComb, Mississippi for voter registration, and the strategic logic: power in Congress sat with white Southern committee chairs from heavily Black counties where Black people did not vote. SNCC targeted Dougherty, Terrell, and Baker counties in Southwest Georgia and Amite, Pike, and Walthall counties in Southwest Mississippi to dislodge senators like John Stennis, James Eastland, Robert Byrd, and Herman Talmadge.

chapters.title: Blood Price and the Pledge
chapters.summary: McDew describes SNCC's internal pledge that violence would be the price of voter-registration work and that beatings, bombings, or killings would not turn them away. He frames this as the key SNCC commitment that distinguished the organization from prior generations who had been scared off by violence.
related_events: SNCC voter-registration violence in Mississippi and Georgia

chapters.title: Education and the Unfinished Struggle
chapters.summary: McDew closes with reflections on education as the key to the future, his speech at Colby College for the Posse Foundation, President Obama's donation of his Nobel Peace Prize money to the Posse Foundation, the Mississippi public-school requirement to study civil rights, the forthcoming Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, and the continuing struggle for democracy.
